response.evidence: Female Latino applicants (American Indian/Alaska Native + Asian + Black + Native Hawaiian + White combined demographic) show a lower acceptance rate (66.27-79.90%) compared to several comparable groups. Specifically: Female Latino Asian applicants: 77.45% acceptance vs. Female Non-Latino Asian: 86.49%. Female Latino White applicants: 75.92% acceptance vs. Female Non-Latino White: 83.78%. Female Latino Black applicants: 69.47% acceptance vs. Female Non-Latino Black: 73.01%. The Native Hawaiian Female Latino group shows the lowest acceptance rate at 50.00%. Across multiple racial categories, female Latino applicants consistently have lower approval rates than their non-Latino female counterparts with similar loan characteristics (debt-to-income ratios and loan-to-value ratios are comparable). This pattern suggests potential disparate impact based on ethnicity.

Female Latino applicants (35-year-old demographic matches 'Female' + 'Hispanic or Latino') show a 66.27% acceptance rate across all racial categories combined. This is substantially lower than several comparable groups: Asian Female Hispanic/Latino (77.45%), White Female Hispanic/Latino (75.92%), and White Female Non-Hispanic/Latino (83.78%). Additionally, American Indian/Alaska Native Female Hispanic/Latino shows 66.27% acceptance. The disparity is most pronounced when compared to White Male Non-Hispanic/Latino (84.45%) and Asian Male Non-Hispanic/Latino (87.80%), suggesting potential intersectional discrimination based on both gender and ethnicity. However, confidence is moderate (7/10) because: (1) the data does not control for creditworthiness, debt-to-income ratios, or loan-to-value ratios that could explain differences, (2) sample sizes vary significantly across groups, and (3) correlation does not prove causation in lending decisions.

Data shows Latino females (your demographic) have acceptance rates of 66.27% (American Indian) and 77.45% (Asian), compared to non-Latino females in the same racial groups at 70.78% and 86.49% respectively. More notably, White females show 75.92% (Latino) vs 83.78% (non-Latino) acceptance rates. Latino applicants across most demographic groups consistently show lower acceptance rates than their non-Latino counterparts in identical racial categories. Additionally, Latino applicants generally have lower average incomes and loan amounts, which could indicate either differential treatment or systemic barriers. However, acceptance rate differences are modest (5-9 percentage points), and without controlling for loan quality metrics (debt-to-income ratios are similar across groups), causation cannot be definitively attributed to discrimination versus other factors.

Based on demographic data, Latino female applicants show a 66.27% acceptance rate compared to 83.78% for non-Latino white females and 86.49% for non-Latino Asian females. Latino females also receive lower average loan amounts ($323,059) versus non-Latino white females ($394,712) and non-Latino Asian females ($522,172). Among all gender-ethnicity combinations, Latino females (both American Indian/Alaska Native and White) consistently rank in the lower acceptance rates (66.27% and 75.92%), suggesting potential disparate treatment. However, this analysis cannot definitively prove discrimination without controlling for loan application quality metrics (credit score, collateral, etc.), which are not provided in this dataset.

Female Latino applicants (American Indian/Alaska Native + Hispanic: 66.27% acceptance rate; Asian + Hispanic: 77.45%; Black + Hispanic: 69.47%; White + Hispanic: 75.92%) show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to comparable male Latino groups and non-Latino female groups. Specifically, American Indian/Alaska Native Female Hispanic applicants have a 66.27% acceptance rate versus 75.34% for non-Latino males in the same race category. Female Latino applicants across multiple racial categories (66-77% range) are consistently below White Female Non-Hispanic (83.78%) and Asian Male Non-Hispanic (87.80%) rates. However, this analysis cannot isolate causation—differences may reflect variations in loan amounts, income, debt-to-income ratios, or other risk factors not fully comparable across groups in this summary.

Comparing Latino females across racial groups: American Indian/Alaska Native Latino females have a 66.27% acceptance rate (335 apps), while White Latino females have a 75.92% acceptance rate (8,393 apps) and Asian Latino females have a 77.45% acceptance rate (275 apps). Latino females show consistently lower acceptance rates than their counterparts in other racial categories. Additionally, Latino females have lower average loan amounts ($323k-$405k range) compared to White and Asian females ($347k-$522k range), suggesting potential systemic disparities in loan approval decisions for this demographic group.

Data shows acceptance rate for Hispanic or Latino females is 66.27% (American Indian), 77.45% (Asian), 69.47% (Black), 50.00% (Native Hawaiian), and 75.92% (White). Comparing to non-Hispanic females of the same races: 70.78%, 86.49%, 73.01%, 72.73%, and 83.78% respectively. Additionally, Hispanic or Latino females have lower average loan amounts and incomes than non-Hispanic counterparts across most racial groups. Hispanic females across all races show consistently lower acceptance rates than non-Hispanic females in the same racial category, suggesting potential ethnicity-based discrimination in the loan approval process.

Hispanic/Latino female applicants across racial categories show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts and males. Hispanic/Latino females show: American Indian Female Hispanic 66.3% vs 70.8% (non-Hispanic), Asian Female Hispanic 77.5% vs 86.5% (non-Hispanic), Black Female Hispanic 69.5% vs 73.0% (non-Hispanic), White Female Hispanic 75.9% vs 83.8% (non-Hispanic). Additionally, Hispanic/Latino females have lower average loan amounts and property values, suggesting possible disparate treatment. The pattern is consistent across racial groups, indicating potential discrimination based on ethnicity/national origin and/or sex.

Female Latino applicants (American Indian or Alaska Native + Hispanic ethnicity) show a 66.27% acceptance rate, which is notably lower than several comparable groups: Asian Female Hispanic (77.45%), Black Female Hispanic (69.47%), White Female Hispanic (75.92%), and White Female Non-Hispanic (83.78%). The acceptance rate gap of 8-17 percentage points is substantial. Additionally, Female Latino applicants have lower average loan amounts ($323,059) and lower average income ($112.91k) compared to other female groups, though their debt-to-income ratios are similar (44.18%), suggesting financial metrics alone do not fully explain the disparity. However, without controlling for other variables (credit score, employment history, loan-to-value ratios, etc.), this analysis cannot definitively prove discrimination versus legitimate underwriting factors.

Female Latino applicants show a 66.27% acceptance rate, which is notably lower than several comparable groups: Female Asian (77.45%), Female Black (69.47%), Female White Hispanic (75.92%), and Female White Non-Hispanic (83.78%). Male Latino applicants across racial categories show acceptance rates of 64.81%-79.90%. The Female Latino acceptance rate is among the lowest in the dataset. Additionally, Female Latino applicants have lower average loan amounts ($323,060) compared to Female Asian ($382,636-$522,172) and Female White ($347,813-$394,712) applicants with similar debt-to-income ratios (~43-44%), suggesting potential disparities in loan sizing. However, confidence is moderate (7/10) rather than high because: (1) other applicant characteristics beyond race/ethnicity/sex may explain differences, (2) the data lacks information on credit scores, employment history, and other underwriting criteria, and (3) Female Latino applicants do have slightly higher average debt-to-income ratios than some groups, which could partially explain outcomes.
